subject: Chemistry
q: In the above conversion the correct sequence of reagents to be added is
question: In the above conversion the correct sequence of reagents to be added is
solution: The required product can be obtained by the following method. In this method nitro toluene is subjected to bromination, bromo group is substituted at ortho position to the methyl as it is more activating group. Then nitro group is reduced to amine group with iron metal in acid. The amine group undergoes diazotisation with nitrous acid. The diazonium group is replaced with chloro group in the presence of cuprous chloride. Finally, methyl group is oxidised in the presence of potassium permanganate.

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of amine compounds from the following giving solids which are soluble in \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) upon reaction with Hinsberg's reagent is _______
question: Number of amine compounds from the following giving solids which are soluble in \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) upon reaction with Hinsberg's reagent is _______
solution: Primary amine give an ionic solid upon reaction with Hinsberg reagent which is soluble in $\mathrm{NaOH}$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are some nitrogen containing compounds
Each of them is treated with HCl separately. 1.0 g of the most basic compound will consume _______ mg of HCl. (Given molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5\))
question:
Given below are some nitrogen containing compounds
Each of them is treated with HCl separately. 1.0 g of the most basic compound will consume _______ mg of HCl. (Given molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5\))
solution:
Benzyl Amine is most basic due to localised lone pair.
Mole of benzyl Amine $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{107}=0.00934$ mole 1 Mole of Benzyl amine consumed 1 mole of HCl So, Mole of HCl consumed $\rightarrow 0.00934$ mole Mass of HCl consumed $\rightarrow 0.00934 \times$ molar mass of HCl $\begin{aligned} & =0.00934 \times 36.5 \\ & =0.341 \mathrm{gm} \\ & =341 \mathrm{mg} \end{aligned}$

question: An organic compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> on treatment with benzene sulfonyl chloride gives compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is soluble in dil. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> solution. solution: Hinsberg reagent (Benzene sulfonyl chloride) gives reaction product with 1° amine, and it is soluble in dil. NaOH.

q: An organic compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>]</mo><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>11</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, shows optical activity and gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> gas on treatment with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>HNO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>]</mo></math> reacts with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>PhSO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi></math> producing a compound which is soluble in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>KOH</mi></math>. The structure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is :
question: An organic compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>]</mo><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>11</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, shows optical activity and gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> gas on treatment with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>HNO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>]</mo></math> reacts with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>PhSO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi></math> producing a compound which is soluble in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>KOH</mi></math>. The structure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is :
solution:
Given:
1. Compound is optically active ⇒ contains chiral carbon/Nitrogen.
2. Compound liberates N2 gas with HNO2⇒ It is primary amine
3. Compound reacts with PhSO2Cl and the product is soluble in KOH - It is a primary amine.

Option (A) and (D) are primary amines. But only option (D) satisfy all the above conditions.

subject: Chemistry
q: From \(6.55 \mathrm{~g}\) of aniline, the maximum amount of acetanilide that can be prepared will be ______ \(\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~g}\).
question: From \(6.55 \mathrm{~g}\) of aniline, the maximum amount of acetanilide that can be prepared will be ______ \(\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~g}\).
solution: $93 \mathrm{~g}$ aniline form $135 \mathrm{gm}$ acetanlide so $\quad 6.55 \mathrm{~g}$ anilne form $\frac{135}{93} \times 6.55=9.5$ $95 \times 10^{-1}$
